title: Extracellular Potassium Relays Axonal Activity to Oligodendrocytes via Kir4.1 Channels to Sustain Axonal Metabolism
abstract_bullets: Mature oligodendrocytes (OLs) in optic nerve sense high-frequency axonal firing through activity-evoked elevations of extracellular potassium (K+).

K+ depolarization of OLs drives Ca2+ influx primarily via reverse-mode Na+/Ca2+ exchanger (NCX), with only minor contributions from ionotropic glutamate and purinergic receptors and no appreciable role for voltage-gated Ca2+ channels.
Blocking OL Kir channels with Ba2+ reduces the 50 Hz stimulation-evoked OL Ca2+ surge by ~80% and the K+-evoked Ca2+ rise by ~88%.
Oligodendrocyte-specific deletion of Kir4.1 (Kir4.1 cKO) slows compound action potential (CAP) recovery after high-frequency stimulation and causes axonopathy at 7-8 months of age.
Kir4.1 loss from OLs reduces the abundance of MCT1 and GLUT1 in central myelin and decreases both basal and activity-induced axonal glucose consumption and lactate availability.

Axonal ATP recovery after chemical ischemia and 50 Hz stimulation is modestly impaired in Kir4.1 cKO, indicating that oligodendroglial K+ uptake links axonal electrical activity to metabolic support before overt neurodegeneration.

sections.title: Introduction
key_points: Myelinating oligodendrocytes provide trophic and metabolic support to axons in addition to accelerating conduction.
Axon-oligodendrocyte communication has been proposed through glutamate and ATP, but acute sensors of axonal firing in mature OLs remain incompletely defined.
Extracellular K+ transients accompany action potentials and Kir4.1 channels on oligodendrocytes and astrocytes are known to clear K+.
Here we test whether K+ elevations themselves relay axonal spiking activity to OLs via Kir4.1 and whether this coupling regulates axonal metabolism and long-term integrity.

subsections.title: High-frequency axonal firing elicits Ca2+ surges in mature oligodendrocytes
OL Ca2+ responses depend on Kir channel-mediated K+ sensing and reverse-mode NCX
OL-specific Kir4.1 deletion slows K+ clearance and causes late-onset axonopathy
Kir4.1 loss from OLs reduces MCT1 and GLUT1 in CNS myelin
Axonal ATP recovery is modestly impaired in Kir4.1 cKO mice
Axonal lactate supply and glucose metabolism depend on oligodendroglial Kir4.1

sections.title: Discussion
paragraphs: K+ as a fast axon-to-OL signal complementing slower glutamatergic/purinergic modes.
Mechanism: Kir4.1-gated depolarization driving reverse-mode NCX in OLs.
Metabolic coupling: Kir4.1 loss reduces MCT1/GLUT1 and axonal glycolysis, reframing oligodendroglial K+ homeostasis as a metabolic regulator.
Implications for demyelinating and neurodegenerative diseases where Kir4.1 is downregulated.
Limitations and outlook, including sustained K+/Ca2+ signalling in vivo and translation to myelin pathologies.

sections.title: Methods
key_points: PLP-CreERT;RCL-GCaMP6s mice, tamoxifen-induced at 6-8 weeks; acute optic nerve preparation with two-photon imaging combined with suction-electrode electrophysiology.
Intravitreal AAV-Cre into RCL-GCaMP6s for axonal GCaMP6s, ATeam1.03 (ATP) and Laconic (lactate) FRET sensors, and FLII12Pglu-700uδ6 (glucose).
Kir4.1 cKO: Kcnj10^fl/fl;MOGiCre; littermates Kcnj10^fl/fl as controls.
Pharmacology: Ba2+, TTX, EGTA, KB-R7943, SEA0400, NBQX, D-AP5, 7-CKA, PPADS, Suramin, Cd2+, Ni2+, nifedipine, benidipine, RuR, bumetanide, ouabain, iodoacetate, cytochalasin B, NaN3.
Proteomics of optic nerve; immunoblotting of purified CNS myelin; electron microscopy for g-ratio and axonal ultrastructure; GFAP/IBA1 immunolabeling.
Statistics: paired/unpaired t-tests, one- and two-way ANOVA with Holm-Šídák or Tukey post-hoc; moderated t-tests for proteomics.
